comments:
Summary
This paper addresses blind deblurring for human faces. The authors propose a learning-based approach built on a modified ResNet, introduce weak supervision via facial landmark localisation as a preprocessing step, and describe an automated pipeline to harvest a large face-frame dataset (2MF2, ≈2.1M frames) used for training. They report results on synthetically blurred images (Gaussian), simulated motion blur by averaging high-frame-rate sequences, and qualitative results on real-world blurred faces, and compare against a number of recent deblurring methods.

Strengths
- Tackles a well-motivated, narrowly-scoped problem (face deblurring) where domain priors can help.
- Use of weak alignment (landmarks) as an inductive bias is sensible and may improve robustness on faces.
- The large semi-automatic dataset (2MF2) is a valuable engineering contribution if made available.
- The approach is lightweight at inference (reported ≈6 fps on Titan X) and uses modern building blocks (ResNet, Huber loss).

Weaknesses and concerns
- Missing architectural and training detail: the paper states modifications to ResNet (disable pooling, add skip connections, batch-norm in skips) but lacks exact layer/parameter descriptions (input/output sizes, number of blocks, stride choices, total parameters). This hinders reproducibility.
- Limited ablation/analysis: there is no ablation that isolates the benefit of the landmark-based weak supervision (e.g., same network trained without landmark pre-processing), nor analysis of the impact of the large dataset vs. smaller training sets.
- Evaluation is not sufficiently thorough: the quantitative evaluation appears limited (PSNR/SSIM on 70 AFLW images under Gaussian blur and some motion-blur simulations). There is no large-scale quantitative benchmark against other methods on standardized datasets with known ground truth. Real-world comparisons are only qualitative (understandable) but should be complemented with more systematic tests.
- Generalisation concerns: training with synthetic blur (Gaussian, motion kernels) may not fully capture real imaging artifacts (nonlinear ψ). The paper acknowledges this but stops short of demonstrating robustness across a wide variety of real blur types, illuminations, occlusions, extreme poses, and low-resolution faces.
- Use of external modules: the system relies on an off-the-shelf detector/tracker/landmark localiser. The manuscript should characterise sensitivity to failure modes of these upstream components (e.g., inaccurate landmarks on heavy blur) and how those errors propagate.
- Lack of public release statement: the manuscript does not state whether the 2MF2 dataset and trained models will be released, which affects the practical impact of the dataset contribution.
- Presentation issues: there are duplicated/repeated passages in the text (several repeated sentences in the conclusion/intro), and some parts read as terse; more careful editing is needed.

Recommendations for improvement
1. Provide full architectural details (layer shapes, filter sizes, strides, activation maps) and training hyperparameters to permit reproducibility. Clarify whether the network outputs only the cropped face and how results are blended into the original image.
2. Add ablations: (a) train the same network without landmark-based preprocessing to quantify its benefit; (b) report performance with and without pretraining and with subsets of the dataset to show the value of 2M+ frames.
3. Broaden quantitative evaluation: run comparisons on more images with ground-truth (e.g., more sequences using high-frame-rate cameras where middle frames act as ground truth), report standard metrics across multiple blur types, and include runtime and model size comparisons.
4. Analyse robustness to landmark localisation errors, occlusions, extreme poses, and very low-resolution faces. Include qualitative failure cases.
5. State whether code and the 2MF2 dataset will be released; if so, include a plan for release.

Conclusion
The paper presents an interesting application of deep networks and weak alignment for face deblurring and contributes a large collected dataset. However, the manuscript lacks sufficient architectural detail, ablation studies, and thorough quantitative evaluation to fully support its claims. With stronger empirical analysis, clearer presentation, and release of data/models, this work would be a useful contribution to the community.
